notes: Wrote zero tool code (fully cold stop) and didn't address the missing PHASE_LOG.json sidecar. Also, this eval's fixture already seeds a populated ISSUES.md template, which may be why baseline reproduces the structured-logging convention here but not in eval 2 (where no template existed yet) -- worth a barer-fixture variant to isolate the skill's actual causal effect.

Pass-rate delta is small (+0.06) because baseline Claude is already competent at the surface behaviors these assertions check (reading files first, drafting a phased plan, pausing on ambiguity, flagging contradictions). The assertions under-measure the skill's actual differentiator, which is structural: exact adherence to the .project/ documentation schema (PROJECT_STATE, PHASE_LOG, ISSUES, ADRs, MODEL_REGISTRY, hybrid MD+JSON), which only pays off across many sessions, not within a single run.

The one assertion baseline failed (eval 2, 'states 3 of 9 progress') is a real, specific gap: baseline never communicates overall progress as a fraction against the full plan, which matters for a user skimming a resume brief after time away.

In both eval 2 and eval 3, with_skill did something the assertions don't check for but that matters a lot for the skill's actual purpose: formally logging newly-discovered problems into ISSUES.md/json with the full template and keeping PROJECT_STATE in sync, versus baseline's without_skill run in eval 2 which only narrated the same discovery in prose and never persisted it. That's the concrete, demonstrable value of 'documentation as mandatory infrastructure' vs. 'documentation as an afterthought.'

Eval 3 is a weaker discriminator than intended: its fixture already seeds a populated ISSUES.md/PHASE_LOG.md example, so baseline Claude picks up the structured-logging convention by imitation rather than by following the skill's instructions. Consider a bare-fixture variant for iteration 2.

with_skill runs take ~1.8x longer and use ~1.6x more tokens on average, driven mostly by reading SKILL.md + references and doing more thorough ground-truth checks (e.g. verifying claimed files actually exist) before responding -- a reasonable cost for the reliability gained, but worth keeping an eye on as the skill grows.
